critic_raw:
The data splitting strategy is the primary methodological concern. The design uses stratified random sampling across 60 months of hospital encounters, explicitly randomizing patients across train, validation, and test sets without temporal separation. For hospital readmission prediction, temporal ordering matters critically: disease coding practices, discharge criteria, hospital protocols, and ICD coding patterns change over time. Training on encounters from 2022 and testing on 2018 encounters — a scenario enabled by random splitting — is methodologically invalid. Real deployment requires predicting future admissions using a model trained on historical data, which temporal splitting enforces.

The multi-task deep learning model uses clinical BERT embeddings concatenated with TF-IDF bag-of-words vectors (top 500 terms). These representations are redundant and do not test a clean architectural hypothesis. More importantly, the baseline logistic regression is trained only on structured features while the multi-task model receives both structured features and two unstructured text representations. This creates a confound: any performance advantage of the multi-task model could stem from the text modality rather than the multi-task architecture. The design claims to test multi-task learning but conflates it with multimodal learning.

The auxiliary task (length-of-stay regression) shares the first two hidden layers but the design does not justify why LOS as an auxiliary task should improve readmission prediction. LOS is highly correlated with severity of illness, but it is also available as a structured input feature. Using LOS as an auxiliary regression target when it is simultaneously available as an input feature creates a circular optimization signal that may trivially improve training convergence without adding genuine predictive value.

Stratified random splitting does not adequately control for repeated patients. The 60-month window covering 100,000 encounters will include multiple encounters per patient. Random splitting can place a patient's month-3 encounter in training and month-48 encounter in test, creating patient-level information leakage.
all_issues_raised: Random split across 60 months violates temporal ordering needed for valid readmission prediction; training on future data and testing on past encounters is methodologically invalid

Confounded comparison: multi-task model receives structured + text features while logistic regression baseline receives only structured features, conflating multi-task learning with multimodal learning
Length-of-stay auxiliary task is circular: LOS is simultaneously an input feature and the auxiliary regression target, creating redundant optimization signal
Patient-level leakage: stratified random split does not prevent multiple encounters per patient from appearing in both training and test sets
TF-IDF + clinical BERT concatenation is redundant; no ablation separates text modality contribution from multi-task architecture contribution

Patient-level leakage: stratified random sampling across 60 months distributes the same patients' multiple encounters across training and test sets, allowing the model to effectively memorize patient-specific profiles

Unspecified NLP preprocessing fit point: it is unclear whether text vectorizers (TF-IDF, vocabulary builders) are fit on the full dataset or training only, creating potential text-level preprocessing leakage

Temporal leakage via auxiliary task labels: if post-discharge treatment efficacy labels require post-discharge observations, using them as auxiliary training targets introduces future information unavailable at prediction time

Metric selection gap: AUROC is used as primary metric for a class-imbalanced task without reporting Precision-Recall AUC or operating point calibration, which are more informative for clinical resource allocation decisions

Single-site generalizability concern: all 100,000 encounters are from a single hospital system over 60 months with no external validation, and no subgroup analysis by department or admission type is specified to assess internal consistency

Stratified random split across 60 months of EHR data does not respect temporal ordering, allowing future patients' data to appear in the training set and inflating performance relative to prospective deployment

The multi-task model receives clinical notes (BERT embeddings + TF-IDF) while the logistic regression baseline receives only structured features, confounding architecture comparison with feature-set comparison
Clinical BERT fine-tuning protocol is ambiguous — if fine-tuned end-to-end, this must be confirmed to use training data only, but the design does not state this explicitly
No ablation study to isolate whether MTL gains come from the auxiliary LOS task, the richer feature set, or the transformer architecture

Patients may appear multiple times across the 60-month window; the design excludes index admissions via rolling window but does not address whether the same patient can appear in both train and test sets
all_issues_adjudicated: Random split across 60 months violates temporal ordering needed for valid readmission prediction; training on future data and testing on past encounters is methodologically invalid
